campaign.title: Anandha Ganesh вҖ” 15 Days of Gen AI
campaign.goal: Establish Gen AI voice + attract interviews/job offers

posts.theme: The Spark вҖ” Why I started Gen AI
posts.content:
6 months ago, I didn't know what a token was.

Today, I've built a system where 7 AI agents collaborate in real-time to research, write, and validate startup ideas вҖ” without me touching a single line of the final output.

Here's how that transformation happened рҹ‘Ү

I'm a developer who was frustrated with building tools that needed constant human input at every step.

Then I discovered Generative AI вҖ” not just ChatGPT as a chat toy, but as a programmable reasoning engine.

I started with one question:
"What if software could THINK about a problem, not just execute instructions?"

That curiosity led me down a 90-day rabbit hole:
вҶ’ Prompt engineering fundamentals
вҶ’ LLM APIs (Gemini, HuggingFace, OpenAI)
вҶ’ RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
вҶ’ Agent orchestration patterns
вҶ’ Multi-agent collaborative systems

Starting today, I'm sharing 15 days of raw, honest learnings вҖ” the concepts, the code, the failures, and the breakthroughs.

If you're a developer curious about where AI is really heading вҖ” follow along.
If you're a hiring manager looking for someone who builds real AI systems вҖ” stay tuned рҹҳҠ

Day 1 of 15. Let's go. рҹҡҖ

#GenAI #MachineLearning #AI #BuildInPublic #SoftwareEngineering #LLM #AIEngineering

posts.theme: Prompt Engineering вҖ” The skill nobody talks about enough
posts.content:
Prompt engineering is not typing questions into ChatGPT.

It's designing instructions that make a language model reason exactly the way you need it to. And it's the highest-leverage skill I picked up in Gen AI.

Here are 5 techniques that actually changed how I build AI systems рҹ‘Ү

рҹ”№ 1. Role Assignment
Bad: "Write a market analysis"
Good: "You are a McKinsey senior analyst with 15 years in SaaS. Write a competitive market analysis for..."
Result: 10x more structured, opinionated output.

рҹ”№ 2. Chain-of-Thought Prompting
Adding "Think step by step" or breaking tasks into reasoning steps dramatically reduces hallucination.

рҹ”№ 3. Output Format Constraints
Tell the LLM EXACTLY what format to return:
"Return ONLY a valid JSON object with keys: title, summary, score. No extra text."

рҹ”№ 4. Few-Shot Examples
Giving the model 2-3 examples of what good output looks like produces far more consistent results than zero-shot prompting.

рҹ”№ 5. Self-Critique Loops
After generating output, prompt the model to critique its own work. The revision is almost always better.

This last one is exactly what my Nova agent does in my AutoGen Studio project вҖ” it audits the AI-generated report before the final draft is published.

Prompt engineering is the difference between a demo and a production AI system.

What prompt technique has worked best for you? Drop it below рҹ‘Ү

#PromptEngineering #GenAI #LLM #AIEngineering #MachineLearning #AI #SoftwareDevelopment

I read 5 articles about LLMs and still didn't understand them.

Then I wrote 9 lines of code and everything clicked.

Here's the developer-first explanation nobody gives you рҹ‘Ү

A Large Language Model is a statistical pattern machine trained on most of the internet's text.

When you send it a prompt, it's not "thinking" вҖ” it's predicting: "Given everything I've seen, what token (word/piece) comes next?"

But here's where it gets interesting for builders:

рҹ§  The model has compressed world knowledge into weights
вҶ’ This means it "knows" how experts write, reason, and conclude

рҹ“җ Context Window = Working Memory
вҶ’ Everything you send in one API call is the model's entire "awareness"
вҶ’ Modern models (Gemini 1.5 Pro) support 1M+ tokens вҖ” that's essentially a whole codebase

рҹ”Ң Tool Calling = LLMs with hands
вҶ’ You can give an LLM access to functions (search web, run code, query DB)
вҶ’ It decides WHEN to call them and WHAT to pass
вҶ’ This is the foundation of AI agents

рҹ”— The magic isn't any single LLM вҖ” it's orchestration
вҶ’ Chaining multiple specialized LLM calls
вҶ’ Passing context between them intelligently
вҶ’ That's what multi-agent systems are about

Once I understood LLMs as programmable reasoning engines вҖ” not chatbots вҖ” everything clicked.

What's one AI concept that confused you before it suddenly made sense?

#LLM #GenAI #AIEngineering #MachineLearning #AI #TechEducation #BuildInPublic

posts.theme: My first real API call вҖ” Google Gemini
posts.content:
9 lines of code.

That's ALL it takes to call one of the most powerful AI models on the planet.

Most developers assume it's complicated. It's not. Here's exactly what it looks like рҹ‘Ү

Here's what that looked like with Google Gemini API рҹ‘Ү

```javascript
const response = await fetch(
  `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-2.0-flash:generateContent?key=${API_KEY}`,
  {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      contents: [{ parts: [{ text: systemPrompt + userMessage }] }],
      generationConfig: { temperature: 0.7, maxOutputTokens: 2048 }
    })
  }
);
```

That's it. That's the bridge between natural language and software.

What I learned from going raw API instead of using SDKs:
вҶ’ You understand exactly what tokens cost
вҶ’ You control temperature (creativity vs determinism)
вҶ’ You learn what "grounding" actually means (Gemini's Google Search integration)
вҶ’ Error handling becomes your best friend

Gemini's built-in Google Search grounding was a game-changer for my research agents вҖ” they could verify facts in real time, not just hallucinate from training data.

I chose Gemini because:
вң… Generous free tier for development
вң… Native Google Search integration
вң… 1M token context window on 1.5 Pro
вң… Gemini 2.0 Flash is blazing fast for agentic workflows

Today's challenge if you're learning: Make ONE raw API call to Gemini. No SDK. Just fetch. You'll learn more in 10 minutes than reading docs for an hour.

#GoogleGemini #GenAI #LLM #AIEngineering #API #BuildInPublic #JavaScript #SoftwareDevelopment

posts.theme: Multi-Agent AI Systems вҖ” The future of software
posts.content:
I gave one AI agent 4 tasks at once.

The output was mediocre.

Then I gave each task to a DIFFERENT AI agent, specialized for exactly that one job.

The difference was night and day. Here's why рҹ‘Ү

A single LLM call has limits:
вқҢ Can only focus on one instruction set
вқҢ Context gets polluted when doing multiple tasks
вқҢ No specialization вҖ” jack of all trades, master of none
вқҢ No quality control

A multi-agent system fixes all of this:

рҹҸ—пёҸ Each agent has ONE job and does it brilliantly
вҶ’ Like a well-run company with specialized departments

рҹ”„ Agents pass context to each other
вҶ’ Agent A's output becomes Agent B's input
вҶ’ Information accumulates, not gets lost

вң… Agents can check each other's work
вҶ’ A "critic" agent reviews a "writer" agent's output
вҶ’ Quality improves automatically through adversarial review

рҹҢҗ Agents can use different models
вҶ’ Use Gemini 2.0 Flash for fast reasoning
вҶ’ Use Qwen 2.5 72B for deep analysis
вҶ’ Pick the right model for the right job

The pattern I use in my project:
User Input вҶ’ Orchestrator вҶ’ Agent 1 (research) вҶ’ Agent 2 (structure) вҶ’ Agent 3 (write) вҶ’ Agent 4 (critique) вҶ’ Final Output

This is how serious AI products are built in 2025.
Not one ChatGPT call. Coordinated agent teams.

I'll reveal my exact architecture tomorrow. Stay tuned рҹ‘Җ

#MultiAgentAI #AIAgents #GenAI #AIEngineering #LLM #MachineLearning #SoftwareArchitecture

posts.content:

posts.theme: AutoGen Studio вҖ” Full Project Launch рҹҡҖ
posts.content:
I typed one sentence.

7 AI agents went to work.

45 seconds later: full market analysis, competitive teardown, tech blueprint, and a brutal Go/No-Go verdict.

Here's exactly how I built the system that does this рҹ‘Ү

AutoGen Studio runs two complete AI agent teams:

рҹ“қ REPORT COMPILER TEAM
вҶ’ Dr. Atlas: Researches facts, statistics, references across the web
вҶ’ Skye: Structures raw data into a chapter-by-chapter outline  
вҶ’ Sterling: Expands outline into a full descriptive Markdown report
вҶ’ Nova: Quality Inspector вҖ” audits and critiques the draft
вҶ’ Sterling performs a final edit based on Nova's feedback

рҹҡҖ STARTUP VALIDATOR TEAM
вҶ’ Valerie: Researches competitors, market size, customer demographics
вҶ’ Pax: Evaluates tech stack complexity and scaling blueprints
вҶ’ Damien: Devil's Advocate вҖ” brutally critiques every flaw
вҶ’ Vera: Investment Partner вҖ” delivers SWOT matrix + Go/No-Go score

в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
вҡҷпёҸ TECH STACK
в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
вҶ’ Frontend: React (Vite) + Custom dark mode UI with glassmorphism
вҶ’ Backend: Node.js + Express
вҶ’ LLMs: Google Gemini 2.0 Flash / 1.5 Pro + HuggingFace (Qwen 2.5 72B, LLaMA 3.3)
вҶ’ Orchestration: Custom sequential promise-chain state machine
вҶ’ Database: JSON-based persistent storage

в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ

в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ
Building the orchestration engine that:
- Passes context between agents without losing information
- Handles streaming responses in real-time
- Manages agent "personalities" through system prompts
- Switches between Gemini and HuggingFace seamlessly

This is what 90 days of learning Gen AI looks like when you build instead of just consume.

posts.theme: How AI agents actually talk to each other
posts.content:
After yesterday's post, one question kept coming up:

"How do 7 AI agents coordinate without talking over each other?"

The answer is embarrassingly simple вҖ” and it changed how I think about software architecture forever рҹ‘Ү

It's not magic. It's structured context passing.

```
Agent A Output вҶ’ [Context Object] вҶ’ Agent B System Prompt
```

Every agent in my system receives:
1. Its own ROLE (who it is, its expertise, its personality)
2. The TASK (what it needs to produce)
3. PREVIOUS CONTEXT (what agents before it already produced)

Here's a simplified version of how Sterling (the writer) gets context from Skye (the outline architect):

```javascript
const sterlingPrompt = `
  You are Sterling, a lead technical writer.
  
  The Outline Architect has produced this structure:
  ${skyeOutput}
  
  The Researcher provided these raw facts:
  ${atlasOutput}
  
  Your task: Expand this outline into a complete, 
  well-cited report. Maintain all section headers.
  Return only the final report in Markdown.
`;
```

That's it. That's inter-agent communication in its simplest form.

The art is in:
вҶ’ What context to include (too much = token waste, too little = bad output)
вҶ’ How to instruct each agent not to "undo" prior work
вҶ’ How to handle failures gracefully (what if an agent returns garbage?)
вҶ’ Streaming responses to the UI so users see it live

The hardest part wasn't the AI вҖ” it was the orchestration state machine that manages the sequential flow without blocking the event loop.

I built a custom promise-chain system for this in Node.js.

Want me to do a deep dive on the orchestration code? Comment below рҹ‘Ү

#AIAgents #MultiAgentAI #GenAI #AIEngineering #NodeJS #SoftwareArchitecture #LLM #BuildInPublic

I replaced a paid GPT-4 call with a FREE open-source model.

The output was better.

Here's exactly which model, why it worked, and how it changed my entire AI stack рҹ‘Ү

рҹӨ” Why not just use one provider?
Vendor lock-in is real. API costs scale. And some open-source models are genuinely competitive with GPT-4.

In AutoGen Studio, I built a dual-provider system:
- Google Gemini (for speed + Google Search grounding)
- HuggingFace Serverless Inference API (for open-source models at zero server cost)

рҹ”Ҙ Models I tested on HuggingFace:

рҹ“Ҡ Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct вҖ” Alibaba's model
вҶ’ Surprisingly strong at structured analysis tasks
вҶ’ Follows formatting instructions very precisely
вҶ’ Great for the Startup Validator's SWOT analysis

рҹҰҷ LLaMA 3.3 70B вҖ” Meta's model  
вҶ’ Strong reasoning, excellent for long-form writing
вҶ’ The Sterling (writer) agent shines with LLaMA

вҡЎ Mistral 7B вҖ” When you need speed
вҶ’ Fast, cheap, good for quick classification tasks

The HuggingFace Serverless API is remarkable:
вҶ’ No server to manage
вҶ’ Pay per token (or use free tier)
вҶ’ Same unified API format across all models
вҶ’ Switch models with ONE line of code

Key insight: Different agents do better with different models. A 7B model for routing, a 72B model for deep analysis вҖ” that's real cost optimization.

рҹ”‘ You need: A HuggingFace token with "Make calls to Inference Providers" permission.

Have you tried any HuggingFace models in production? Which performed best?

#HuggingFace #OpenSource #GenAI #LLM #Qwen #LLaMA #AIEngineering #MachineLearning

posts.theme: The hardest bug I fixed вҖ” and what it taught me
posts.content:
I spent 2 full days debugging an AI pipeline that was technically working perfectly.

The bug wasn't in the code.

It was in how I was THINKING about AI. Here's what broke вҖ” and the lesson that changed everything рҹ‘Ү

Here's what happened рҹ‘Ү

I had 4 agents running sequentially. Each agent received all previous agents' outputs as context.

Everything worked beautifully... until Agent 4.

Agent 4 (Nova, the quality inspector) would start REWRITING the entire report from scratch instead of critiquing it.

2 days of debugging. Here's what was wrong:

вқҢ The Problem:
I was injecting ALL previous context at full verbosity into Nova's prompt:
вҶ’ Atlas's 2000-word research dump
вҶ’ Skye's 800-word outline
вҶ’ Sterling's 3000-word report

Total context: ~6000 tokens of input BEFORE the instruction.

Nova was so overwhelmed by content that it pattern-matched to "I need to produce content" rather than "I need to critique content."

вң… The Fix:
Summary injection instead of full injection:
вҶ’ Atlas: only key facts list (not full research)
вҶ’ Skye: only section headers (not full outline)
вҶ’ Sterling: full report (because Nova needs to critique this)
вҶ’ Nova's instruction: placed FIRST, not last

Result: Nova now produces laser-focused critiques every single time.

рҹ§  The lesson:
LLMs are not databases. They weight recent tokens more heavily. The ORDER and VOLUME of context matters as much as the content.

"Context engineering" is just as important as prompt engineering.

What's the hardest bug you've squashed in an AI project?

#AIEngineering #Debugging #GenAI #LLM #BuildInPublic #SoftwareDevelopment #MachineLearning #ProblemSolving

posts.theme: What Gemini 2.0 Flash taught me about speed vs quality
posts.content:
My AI pipeline took 16.5 seconds from input to output.

Users were abandoning it before it finished.

One model swap cut it to 7 seconds вҖ” with BETTER quality output.

Here's the counterintuitive move that saved the product рҹ‘Ү

When I started AutoGen Studio, I used Gemini 1.5 Pro for everything.
Reasonable choice вҖ” it's incredibly capable.

But in an agentic system with 4-5 sequential LLM calls, latency compounds:
вҶ’ Agent 1: 3.2s
вҶ’ Agent 2: 4.1s  
вҶ’ Agent 3: 5.8s
вҶ’ Agent 4: 3.4s
= 16.5 seconds for a user to see ANYTHING

That's a dead product.

So I switched research/routing agents to Gemini 2.0 Flash. Here's what happened:

вҡЎ Flash agents: 0.8-1.2s average
рҹ§  Pro agents (writing/analysis only): 3-4s

Total pipeline: ~7 seconds вҖ” and users see streaming output from second 1.

But here's the nuanced insight:

Flash is NOT just a faster, dumber Pro. It's a different model with different strengths:
вҶ’ Flash = Excellent at following instructions precisely and quickly
вҶ’ Pro = Better at nuanced reasoning and long-form synthesis

My hybrid strategy:
вҶ’ Use Flash for: research routing, outline generation, classification, formatting
вҶ’ Use Pro for: final writing, complex analysis, multi-step reasoning

Now my agents feel like a fast, intelligent team instead of a slow, sequential queue.

This is model selection strategy вҖ” one of the most underrated skills in AI engineering.

How do you balance speed vs quality in your AI projects?

#GoogleGemini #GenAI #AIEngineering #LLM #Performance #AI #MachineLearning #SoftwareDevelopment

posts.theme: The Startup Validator вҖ” My favorite feature
posts.content:
I fed my startup idea to an AI agent whose ONLY job is to destroy it.

It found 6 fatal flaws I had completely missed.

Meet Damien вҖ” the most brutally honest feedback I've ever received рҹ‘Ү

You type your idea. Four agents go to work:

рҹ”Қ VALERIE вҖ” Market Scout
"Your target market is ~47M SMBs in India. Key competitors: Razorpay, Instamojo. Differentiation gap exists in..."

вҡҷпёҸ PAX вҖ” Tech Architect  
"MVP requires: React frontend, Node.js API, PostgreSQL. Recommended: Deploy on Railway. Scale risk at 10k users is..."

рҹҳҲ DAMIEN вҖ” Devil's Advocate (my personal favorite)
"Here's what will kill this idea: Customer acquisition cost vs lifetime value math doesn't work. B2B sales cycle in this segment averages 6 months..."

рҹ’° VERA вҖ” Investment Partner
Delivers the final synthesis:
вҶ’ Strengths: [list]
вҶ’ Weaknesses: [list]
вҶ’ Opportunities: [list]  
вҶ’ Threats: [list]
вҶ’ Risk Rating: Medium-High
вҶ’ Investment Verdict: CONDITIONAL GO вң…

What makes Damien special:
I deliberately made him antagonistic. His system prompt instructs him to find every flaw, assume worst-case scenarios, and challenge every assumption.

The best feedback I got on a real startup idea came from Damien вҖ” not from humans who were being polite.

This is what AI can do that humans sometimes can't: give you the unfiltered truth.

рҹ”— Try it yourself: https://github.com/Anandhaganesh/autogen-studio

What startup idea would you want Damien to tear apart? рҹҳӮ

#StartupValidator #GenAI #AIAgents #Startup #AIEngineering #MultiAgentAI #BuildInPublic

posts.theme: 10 lessons from 90 days of building with Gen AI
posts.content:
90 days ago I thought Gen AI was just a smarter search bar.

Today I have 7 AI agents running in production вҖ” built entirely from scratch.

Here are the 10 things I wish someone had told me on Day 1 рҹ‘Ү

1пёҸвғЈ Start with one API call, not a framework
Learn the raw API before adding abstractions. You'll understand what every library actually does.

2пёҸвғЈ LLMs are not deterministic. Design for variation.
The same prompt can return different outputs. Build systems that are resilient to this.

3пёҸвғЈ Temperature is the most underused parameter
Low temp (0.1-0.3) for structured output. High temp (0.7-0.9) for creative writing. Match it to the task.

4пёҸвғЈ Streaming UX is not optional for agents
Users will not wait 15 seconds staring at a spinner. Stream everything.

5пёҸвғЈ Context is your most valuable resource
Every token you put in context is a decision. Be ruthless about what each agent actually needs.

6пёҸвғЈ The critic agent is underrated
Add a "review" step to any AI pipeline. Quality improves dramatically with adversarial self-review.

7пёҸвғЈ Personas make agents perform better
Naming an agent "Damien, the Devil's Advocate" and giving it a personality produces more useful output than saying "critically analyze this."

8пёҸвғЈ Error handling in LLM systems is different
LLMs fail silently вҖ” they return text, just wrong text. Validate outputs structurally, not just for errors.

9пёҸвғЈ Open source models are production-ready
Qwen 2.5 72B performs competitively with GPT-4 on many tasks. Cost savings are significant at scale.

рҹ”ҹ Build something you'd actually use
The best projects solve a problem you personally have. I was tired of shallow research. AutoGen Studio fixed that for me.

Which of these resonates most with you?

#GenAI #AIEngineering #LessonsLearned #MachineLearning #BuildInPublic #SoftwareDevelopment #LLM

posts.theme: The future of AI agents вҖ” my honest take
posts.content:
In 2 years, this one skill will appear on half of all software engineering job descriptions.

Right now, fewer than 1% of developers have it.

Here's what's coming вҖ” and exactly how to position yourself ahead of it рҹ‘Ү

рҹ”® What's coming:

вҶ’ Memory-first agents
Right now, most agents are stateless вҖ” they forget everything after a session. The next wave will have persistent memory, evolving knowledge bases, and long-term user context.

вҶ’ Tool-native agents  
Agents won't just call LLMs вҖ” they'll write code, execute it, read the output, fix the errors, and try again. Autonomously. Without human intervention.

вҶ’ Agent-to-agent marketplaces
Specialized agents (legal research, financial modeling, code review) will be bought and plugged into workflows like APIs today.

вҶ’ Human-in-the-loop by design, not by default
The best systems will include humans for approval checkpoints, not for execution steps.

рҹӨ” What this means for developers:

вҶ’ Prompt engineering is becoming system design
вҶ’ Context engineering is the new database optimization
вҶ’ Agent orchestration patterns are the new design patterns
вҶ’ "AI Engineer" is a real, distinct specialization вҖ” not just a marketing term

I don't think traditional software is going away. But the LEVER that software engineers have just got 10x bigger with AI.

The developers who learn to orchestrate intelligent systems вҖ” not just write functions вҖ” will define the next decade.

I'm actively building in this space. And I'm looking for teams doing the same.

What's your take on the agent future?

#AIAgents #FutureOfAI #GenAI #AIEngineering #MachineLearning #SoftwareEngineering #Tech #OpenToWork
